subject: Mathematics
q:
Let f:ℝ→ℝ be defined as fx=ex,x<0aex+x-1,0≤x<1b+sinπx,1≤x<2e-x-c,x≥2
where a,b,c∈ℝ and t denotes greatest integer less than or equal to t. Then, which of the following statements is true?
question:
Let f:ℝ→ℝ be defined as fx=ex,x<0aex+x-1,0≤x<1b+sinπx,1≤x<2e-x-c,x≥2
where a,b,c∈ℝ and t denotes greatest integer less than or equal to t. Then, which of the following statements is true?
options: There exists <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>b</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>c</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">ℝ</mi></math> such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi></math> is continuous of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">ℝ</mi></math>.

If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi></math> is discontinuous at exactly one point, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>b</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>c</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math>

If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi></math> is discontinuous at exactly one point, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>b</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>c</mi><mo>≠</mo><mn>1</mn></math>.

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi></math> is discontinuous at atleast two points, for any values of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>b</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi></math>.
solution:
Given fx=ex,x<0aex+x-1,0≤x<1b+sinπx,1≤x<2e-x-c,x≥2

i.e. fx=0,x<0aex-1,0≤x<1b,x=1b-1,1<x<2-c,x≥2

Here, we know fx is discontinuous at x=1 as b≠b-1

For fx to be continuous at x=0; a=1

For fx to be continuous at x=2; b+c=1

Hence, a+b+c=2

subject: Mathematics
q:
Statement 1: A function $f: R \rightarrow R$ is continuous at $x_0$ if and only if $\lim _{x \rightarrow x_0} f(x)$ exists and $\lim _{x \rightarrow x_0} f(x)=f\left(x_0 \cdot\right)$
Statement 2: A function $f: R \rightarrow R$ is discontinuous at $x_0$ if and only if, $\lim _{x \rightarrow x_0} f(x)$ exists and $\lim _{x \rightarrow x_0} f(x) \neq f\left(x_0.\right)$
question:
Statement 1: A function $f: R \rightarrow R$ is continuous at $x_0$ if and only if $\lim _{x \rightarrow x_0} f(x)$ exists and $\lim _{x \rightarrow x_0} f(x)=f\left(x_0 \cdot\right)$
Statement 2: A function $f: R \rightarrow R$ is discontinuous at $x_0$ if and only if, $\lim _{x \rightarrow x_0} f(x)$ exists and $\lim _{x \rightarrow x_0} f(x) \neq f\left(x_0.\right)$
options: Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is true, Statement 2 is not a correct explanation of Statement 1.
Statement 1 is false, Statement 2 is true.
Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is true, Statement 2 is a correct explanation of Statement 1.
Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is false.
solution:
Statement-1 is true.
It is the definition of continuity.
Statement $-2$ is false.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $f:[-1,2] \rightarrow \mathbf{R}$ be given by $f(x)=2 x^2+x+\left[x^2\right]-[x]$, where $[t]$ denotes the greatest integer less than or equal to $t$. The number of points, where $f$ is not continuous, is :
question: Let $f:[-1,2] \rightarrow \mathbf{R}$ be given by $f(x)=2 x^2+x+\left[x^2\right]-[x]$, where $[t]$ denotes the greatest integer less than or equal to $t$. The number of points, where $f$ is not continuous, is :
solution:
Doubtful points : $-1,0,1, \sqrt{2}, \sqrt{3}, 2$ at $x=\sqrt{2}, \sqrt{3}$ \(\begin{array}{cc} f(x)=\left(2 x^2+x-[x]\right)+\left[x^2\right]= \text{Discount} \\ \downarrow \qquad\qquad\quad \downarrow \\ \text{Cont.} \qquad\qquad \text{Cont.} \end{array}\)
at $x=-1$ : $\left.\begin{array}{rl}\text { RHL } \Rightarrow & f(x)=(2-1-(-1))+0=2 \\ & f(-1)=2-1-(-1)+1=3\end{array}\right\}$ Dis. at $x=2$ : $\left.\begin{array}{rl} \text {LHL } \Rightarrow f(x) & =8+2-1+3=12 \\ f(2) & =8+2-2+4=12 \end{array}\right\} \text { Cont. }$ at $\mathrm{x}=0$ : $\left.\begin{array}{rl} \text {LHL } \Rightarrow & 0+0-(-1)+0=1 \\ & \mathrm{f}(0)=0 \end{array}\right\} \text { Dis. }$ at $\mathrm{x}=1$ $\left.\begin{array}{rl} \text {LHL } \Rightarrow & 2+1-0+0=3 \\ & f(1)=3-1+1=3 \\ \text {RHL } \Rightarrow & 2+1-1+1=3 \end{array}\right\} \text { Cont. }$

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Given fx=2+x-x-1+x+1, x∈R ⇒fx=-x,x<-1x+2,-1≤x<03x+2,0≤x<1x+4,x≥1

⇒f'x=-1,x<-11,-1<x<03,0<x<11,x>1

∴  f'-32+f'-12+f'12+f'32

=-1+1+3+1=4

i.e. S1 is incorrect.

Now  ∫-22fxdx=∫-2-1fxdx+∫-10fxdx+∫01fxdx+∫12fxdx

=-x22-2-1+x+222-10+3x+22601+x+42212

-12+2+2-12+256-46+18-252

=32+32+72+112=12

i.e. S2 is correct.

subject: Mathematics
q:
Let a,b∈R,b≠0. Defined a function, fx=asinπ2(x−1),     for x≤0tan2x−sin2xbx3,     for x>0

If f is continuous at x=0, then 10-ab is equal to
question:
Let a,b∈R,b≠0. Defined a function, fx=asinπ2(x−1),     for x≤0tan2x−sin2xbx3,     for x>0

If f is continuous at x=0, then 10-ab is equal to
solution:
We have,

fx=asinπ2x−1,    for x≤0tan2x−sin2xbx3,    for  x>0

If function is continuous at x=0, then

LHL=RHL=f0

Now,

LHL=limx→0-asinπ2x−1

⇒LHL=limh→0asinπ20-h−1

⇒LHL=limh→0asinπ2−1

⇒LHL=-a

Now,

RHL=limx→0+ tan2x−sin2xbx3

∵sinx=x−x33!+x55!+……

∵tanx=x+x33+2x515+….

RHL=limx→0+ (2x)33+(2x)36bx3=83+86b=4b

And,

f(0)=asinπ2(0−1)=asin−π2=−a

Then,

RHL=f0

⇒4b=−a⇒−ab=4⇒10−ab=14

subject: Mathematics
q: The number of points where the function <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="" open="{"><mtable columnalign="left"><mtr><mtd><mfenced close="|" open="|"><mrow><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>7</mn></mrow></mfenced></mtd><mtd><mo> </mo><mi>if</mi><mo> </mo><mo>  </mo><mi>x</mi><mo>⩽</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mn>4</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></mtd><mtd><mo> </mo><mi>if</mi><mo> </mo><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo><</mo><mi>x</mi><mo><</mo><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mfenced close="|" open="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mfenced close="|" open="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></mtd><mtd><mo> </mo><mi>if</mi><mo> </mo><mo>  </mo><mi>x</mi><mo>⩾</mo><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mi>t</mi></mfenced></math> denotes the greatest integer <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>⩽</mo><mi>t</mi></math>, is discontinuous is ______
question: The number of points where the function <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="" open="{"><mtable columnalign="left"><mtr><mtd><mfenced close="|" open="|"><mrow><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>7</mn></mrow></mfenced></mtd><mtd><mo> </mo><mi>if</mi><mo> </mo><mo>  </mo><mi>x</mi><mo>⩽</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mn>4</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></mtd><mtd><mo> </mo><mi>if</mi><mo> </mo><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo><</mo><mi>x</mi><mo><</mo><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mfenced close="|" open="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mfenced close="|" open="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></mtd><mtd><mo> </mo><mi>if</mi><mo> </mo><mo>  </mo><mi>x</mi><mo>⩾</mo><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mi>t</mi></mfenced></math> denotes the greatest integer <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>⩽</mo><mi>t</mi></math>, is discontinuous is ______
solution:
Here both the modulus function is continuous in its domain.

At x=-1, 

limx→-1-2x2-3x-7=2 & limx→-1+4x2-1=2

Hence fx is continuous at x=-1

At x=1.

limx→1-4x2-1=2 & limx→1+x+1+x-2=3

Hence, fx is discontinuous at x=1

For x∈-1,1 the function fx is discontinuous when 4x2-1=0/ 1/ 2

i.e when x∈±12,±12,±32

subject: Mathematics
q:
If the function
$f(x)= \begin{cases}\frac{72^x-9^x-8^x+1}{\sqrt{2}-\sqrt{1+\cos x}}, & x \neq 0 \\ a \log _e 2 \log _e 3 & , x=0\end{cases}$
is continuous at $x=0$, then the value of $a^2$ is equal to
question:
If the function
$f(x)= \begin{cases}\frac{72^x-9^x-8^x+1}{\sqrt{2}-\sqrt{1+\cos x}}, & x \neq 0 \\ a \log _e 2 \log _e 3 & , x=0\end{cases}$
is continuous at $x=0$, then the value of $a^2$ is equal to
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} f(x)=a \ln 2 \ln 3 \\ & \lim _{n \rightarrow 0} \frac{72^x-9^x-8^x+1}{\sqrt{2}-\sqrt{1+\cos x}}=\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\left(8^x-1\right)\left(9^x-1\right)}{\sqrt{2}-\sqrt{1+\cos x}} \\ & \lim _{n \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{8^x-1}{x}\right)\left(\frac{9^x-1}{x}\right)\left(\frac{x^2}{1-\cos x}\right)(\sqrt{2}+\sqrt{1+\cos x}) \\ & \therefore \ln 8 \times \ln 9 \times 2 \times 2 \sqrt{2}=24 \sqrt{2} \ln 2 \ln 3 \\ & \therefore a=24 \sqrt{2}, a^2=576 \times 2=1152\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q: If the function <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="" open="{"><mtable columnalign="center left"><mtr><mtd><msub><mi>k</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>π</mi><msup><mo>)</mo><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo></mtd><mtd><mi>x</mi><mo>≤</mo><mi>π</mi></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><msub><mi>k</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi>cos</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo></mtd><mtd><mi>x</mi><mo>></mo><mi>π</mi></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced></math> is twice differentiable, then the ordered pair <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi>k</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>k</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to:
question: If the function <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="" open="{"><mtable columnalign="center left"><mtr><mtd><msub><mi>k</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>π</mi><msup><mo>)</mo><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo></mtd><mtd><mi>x</mi><mo>≤</mo><mi>π</mi></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><msub><mi>k</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi>cos</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo></mtd><mtd><mi>x</mi><mo>></mo><mi>π</mi></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced></math> is twice differentiable, then the ordered pair <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi>k</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>k</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to:
solution:
f(x) is differentiable then will also continuous then f(π)=k1π-π2-1=-1

fπ+=limh→0k2cosπ+h=-k2

fπ+=fπ⇒k2=1…1,f is continuous.

Now, f'(x)=ddxk1(x-π)2,x≤πddxk2cosx,x>π

 f'(x)=2k1(x-π),x≤π-k2sinx,x>π

then, f'π-=2k1π-π=0

f'π+=-k2sinπ=0

 ⇒f'π-=f'π+=0

Similarly, we get

f''(x)=2k1,x≤π-k2cosx,x>π

As the function is twice differentiable, the second-order derivatives, LHD=RHD 

⇒f''π-=f''π+⇒2k1=-k2cosπ

⇒2k1=k2

⇒k1=12 (from 1)

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $f(x)=x|x|, g(x)=\sin x$ and $h(x)=(g \circ f)(x)$. Then
question: Let $f(x)=x|x|, g(x)=\sin x$ and $h(x)=(g \circ f)(x)$. Then
options: $\mathrm{h}(\mathrm{x})$ is differentiable at $\mathrm{x}=0$, but $\mathrm{h}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})$ is not continuous at $x=0$
$\mathrm{h}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})$ is continuous at $\mathrm{x}=0$ but it is not differentiable at $x=0$
solution:
Let $f(x)=x|x|=x|x|, g(x)=\sin x$ and $h(x)=g o f(x)=g[f(x)]$
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\therefore h(x)= \begin{cases}\sin x^2, & x \geq 0 \\
-\sin x^2, & x < 0\end{cases} \\
&\text { Now, } h^{\prime}(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}
2 x \cos x^2, & x \geq 0 \\
-2 x \cos x^2, & x < 0
\end{array}\right.
\end{aligned}
$$
Since, L.H.L and R.H.L at $x=0$ of $h^{\prime}(x)$ is equal to 0 therefore $h^{\prime}(x)$ is continuous at $x=0$
Now, suppose $h^{\prime}(x)$ is differentiable
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\therefore h^{\prime \prime}(x) \\
&= \begin{cases}2\left(\cos x^2+2 x^2\left(-\sin x^2\right),\right. & x \geq 0 \\
2\left(-\cos x^2+2 x^2 \sin x^2\right), & x < 0\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$
Since, L.H.L and R.H.L at $x=0$ of $h^{\prime \prime}(x)$ are different therefore $h^{\prime \prime}(x)$ is not continuous.
$\Rightarrow h^{\prime \prime}(x)$ is not differentiable
$\Rightarrow$ our assumption is wrong
Hence $h^{\prime}(x)$ is not differentiable at $x=0$.

subject: Mathematics
q:
Let $f(x)=x|x|$ and $g(x)=\sin x$.
Statement-1 : gof is differentiable at $x=0$ and its derivative is continuous at that point.
Statement-2 : gof is twice differentiable at $x=0$.
question:
Let $f(x)=x|x|$ and $g(x)=\sin x$.
Statement-1 : gof is differentiable at $x=0$ and its derivative is continuous at that point.
Statement-2 : gof is twice differentiable at $x=0$.
options: Statement-1 is true, Statement-2 is true; Statement-2 is a correct explanation for Statement-1
Statement-1 is true, Statement-2 is true; Statement-2 is not a correct explanation for Statement-1
Statement-1 is true, Statement-2 is false
Statement-1 is false, Statement-2 is true
solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& f(x)=x|x| \text { and } g(x)=\sin x \\
& g \circ f(x)=\sin (x|x|)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}
-\sin x^2 & , x < 0 \\
\sin x^2 & , x \geq 0
\end{array}\right. \\
& \therefore \text { (gof })^{\prime}(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}
-2 x \cos x^2 & , x < 0 \\
2 x \cos x^2 & , x \geq 0
\end{array}\right.
\end{aligned}
$$
Clearly, L(gof )' $(0)=0=\mathrm{R}(\text { gof })^{\prime}(0)$
$\therefore$ gof is differentiable at $x=0$ and also its derivative is continuous at $x=0$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { Now, (gof)" }(\mathrm{x})=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}
-2 \cos \mathrm{x}^2+4 \mathrm{x}^2 \sin \mathrm{x}^2 & , \mathrm{x} < 0 \\
2 \cos \mathrm{x}^2-4 \mathrm{x}^2 \sin \mathrm{x}^2 & , \mathrm{x} \geq 0
\end{array}\right. \\
& \therefore \mathrm{L}(\text { gof })^{\prime \prime}(0)=-2 \text { and } \mathrm{R}(\text { gof })^{\prime \prime}(0)=2 \\
& \therefore \mathrm{L}(\text { gof })^{\prime \prime}(0) \neq \mathrm{R}(\text { gof })^{\prime \prime}(0)
\end{aligned}
$$
$\therefore g o f(x)$ is not twice differentiable at $x=0$.

subject: Mathematics
q: The number of points, where the function <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mo>:</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>→</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfenced open="|" close="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>cos</mi><mfenced open="|" close="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>sin</mi><mfenced open="|" close="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced><mfenced open="|" close="|"><mrow><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>, is NOT differentiable, is
question: The number of points, where the function <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mo>:</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>→</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfenced open="|" close="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>cos</mi><mfenced open="|" close="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>sin</mi><mfenced open="|" close="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced><mfenced open="|" close="|"><mrow><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>, is NOT differentiable, is
solution:
Given,fx=x-1cosx-2sinx-1+x-3x2-5x+4

=x-1cosx-2sinx-1+x-3x-1x-4

=x-1cosx-2sinx-1+x-3x-4

We know that modulus function is not differentiable at values where modulus function is zero

So, we can say that function is non-differentiable at x=1 and x=4.

subject: Mathematics
q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>g</mi><mo>:</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>→</mo><mi>R</mi></math> be two real valued function defined as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="" open="{"><mtable><mtr><mtd><mo>-</mo><mfenced close="|" open="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced></mtd><mtd><mo>,</mo></mtd><mtd><mi>x</mi><mo><</mo><mn>0</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><msup><mi>e</mi><mi>x</mi></msup></mtd><mtd><mo>,</mo></mtd><mtd><mi>x</mi><mo>≥</mo><mn>0</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="" open="{"><mtable columnalign="left"><mtr><mtd><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msub><mi>k</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mi>x</mi></mtd><mtd><mo>,</mo></mtd><mtd><mi>x</mi><mo><</mo><mn>0</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><msub><mi>k</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mtd><mtd><mo>,</mo></mtd><mtd><mi>x</mi><mo>≥</mo><mn>0</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>k</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>k</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> are real constants. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mi>o</mi><mi>f</mi></math> is differentiable at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mi>o</mi><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo></math><math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mi>o</mi><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mn>4</mn></mfenced></math> is equal to
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>g</mi><mo>:</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>→</mo><mi>R</mi></math> be two real valued function defined as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="" open="{"><mtable><mtr><mtd><mo>-</mo><mfenced close="|" open="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced></mtd><mtd><mo>,</mo></mtd><mtd><mi>x</mi><mo><</mo><mn>0</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><msup><mi>e</mi><mi>x</mi></msup></mtd><mtd><mo>,</mo></mtd><mtd><mi>x</mi><mo>≥</mo><mn>0</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="" open="{"><mtable columnalign="left"><mtr><mtd><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msub><mi>k</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mi>x</mi></mtd><mtd><mo>,</mo></mtd><mtd><mi>x</mi><mo><</mo><mn>0</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><msub><mi>k</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mtd><mtd><mo>,</mo></mtd><mtd><mi>x</mi><mo>≥</mo><mn>0</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>k</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>k</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> are real constants. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mi>o</mi><mi>f</mi></math> is differentiable at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mi>o</mi><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo></math><math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mi>o</mi><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mn>4</mn></mfenced></math> is equal to
solution:
Here fx=x+3;x<-3-x+3;-3≤x<0ex;x≥0

and gx=x2+k1x;x<04x+k2;x≥0

Now gfx=fx2+k1fx;fx<04fx+k2;fx≥0

i.e. gfx=x+32+k1x+3;x<-3x+32-k1x+3;-3≤x<04ex+k2;x≥0

For continuity at x=0, 

gof0=gf0-=gf0+

i.e. 4+k2=9-3k1=4+k2

⇒3k1+k2=5     ⋯i

Now differentiating, we get

gfx'=2x+3+k1;x<-32x+3-k1;-3≤x<04ex;x≥0

At x=0,

6-k1=4

⇒k1=2     …ii

∴  k1=2, k2=-1 (from i)

So gofx=x+32+2x+3;x<-3x+32-2x+3;-3≤x<04ex-1;x≥0

Hence, gof-4+gof4=4e4-2=22e4-1

subject: Mathematics
q: The set of points where $f(x)=\frac{x}{1+|x|}$ is differentiable is
question: The set of points where $f(x)=\frac{x}{1+|x|}$ is differentiable is
solution:
$$
f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll}
\frac{x}{1-x}, & x < 0 \\
\frac{x}{1+x}, & x \geq 0
\end{array} \quad \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)= \begin{cases}\frac{1}{(1-x)^2}, & x < 0 \\
\frac{1}{(1+x)^2}, & x \geq 0\end{cases}\right.
$$
$\therefore f^{\prime}(x)$ exist at everywhere.

Then the sum of number of elements in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> is equal to _____.
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mo>:</mo><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>→</mo><mi>R</mi></math> be defined by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mi>min</mi><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mfenced close="]" open="["><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><mfenced close="]" open="["><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mi>x</mi></mfenced></math> is the greatest integer less than or equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> denote the set containing all <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>∈</mo><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi></math> is discontinuous, and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> denote the set containing all <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>∈</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi></math> is not differentiable. Then the sum of number of elements in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> is equal to _____.
solution:
Given,

fx=minx-x,1+x-x

So, the graph of the function is 

1-x=1-x;0≤x<1

From the graph we can say that the function is continuous at all points in the interval 0,3.

So, P=0

Again, from the diagram, we can say that at x=12,1,32,2,52, there are sharp edges.

So, the function is non differentiable at x=12,1,32,2,52

Hence, Q=5

So, P+Q=5
